stem: For \( f(x)=x^{[[m]]} \), find the \( [[n]] \)th derivative \( f^{([[n]])}([[x0]]) \).
solution: Differentiating \( x^{[[m]]} \) \( [[n]] \) times and evaluating at \( [[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).

stem: For \( f(x)=[[a]]e^{[[k]]x} \), find the \( [[n]] \)th derivative \( f^{([[n]])}(0) \).
solution: Each differentiation of \( e^{[[k]]x} \) multiplies by \( [[k]] \), so \( f^{([[n]])}(x)=[[a]][[k]]^{[[n]]}e^{[[k]]x} \); at \( x=0 \) this is \( [[a]]\cdot[[k]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( k \) by \( n \) instead of raising \( k \) to the \( n \)th power.
This raises \( n \) to the \( k \)th power instead of \( k \) to the \( n \)th power.
This stops after only one differentiation instead of \( n \).
This omits the leading coefficient \( a \) entirely.
This is off by \( a \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( a \) from the correct value.

stem: For \( f(x)=[[a]]\sin(x) \), find the \( [[n]] \)th derivative \( f^{([[n]])}(0) \).
solution: The derivatives of \( \sin(x) \) cycle with period 4: \( \sin,\cos,-\sin,-\cos \); at \( x=0 \) these are \( 0,1,0,-1 \). Since \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \) determines the pattern, \( f^{([[n]])}(0)=[[a]]\cdot[[answer]]/[[a]] \) reduces to \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the \( n \)th derivative always lands on \( \cos(0)=1 \) scaled by \( a \).
This assumes the \( n \)th derivative always lands on \( -\cos(0)=-1 \) scaled by \( a \).
This assumes the pattern never lands on a cosine term, always giving 0.
This multiplies by \( n \) instead of using the mod-4 cyclic pattern.
This is off by \( a \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( a \) from the correct value.

stem: For \( f(x)=\dfrac{1}{x+[[c]]} \), find the \( [[n]] \)th derivative \( f^{([[n]])}([[x0]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the correct denominator power but drops the alternating sign \( (-1)^n \).
This drops the exponent-plus-one shift but keeps the correct sign, then double-counts the sign.
This drops the alternating sign \( (-1)^n \) while keeping the correct denominator power.
This forgets the factorial factor \( n! \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Since \( f \) is a degree-\( [[m]] \) polynomial, every derivative of order greater than \( [[m]] \) is identically 0, so \( f^{([[m]]+[[n]])}(x)\equiv 0 \), giving \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the leading coefficient, mistakenly treated as surviving past the polynomial's degree.
This is \( a\cdot m! \), the value of the \( m \)th derivative, not a derivative of higher order which is 0.
This assumes the derivative pattern keeps reproducing a linear term instead of vanishing.
This assumes some nonzero constant survives instead of recognizing the derivative is identically 0.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
